verses.commentary: Lifelong darkness. Jesus initiates the encounter. Divine compassion sees need.
Rabbi, who sinned, this man or his parents, that he was born blind? Disciples assume sin causes suffering. Retribution theology. The question reveals limited understanding of God's purposes.

Neither this man nor his parents sinned, but this happened so that the works of God might be displayed in him. Jesus rejects simplistic cause. Suffering serves divine glory. Not punishment but platform for revelation.
Urgency of mission. Daylight represents Jesus' earthly ministry. Death approaches.
Temporary presence. Light imagery from chapter 8 continues. Jesus illuminates spiritually and physically.
Saliva considered healing in ancient world. Mud recalls creation (Genesis 2:7). Re-creation imagery.

Go, wash in the Pool of Siloam (this word means Sent). So the man went and washed, and came home seeing. Obedience required. Siloam means Sent, pointing to Jesus the Sent One. Faith acts on command. Washing brings sight. Baptismal overtones.
Transformation so radical it creates doubt. Change challenges recognition.
The healed man testifies to his identity. Transformation creates testimony.
How then were your eyes opened? The question everyone asks. Explanation demanded. Miracle requires accounting.

The man they call Jesus made some mud and put it on my eyes. He told me to go to Siloam and wash. So I went and washed, and then I could see. Simple testimony. He recounts facts. Jesus identified as agent. Obedience led to sight.
Where is this man? I don't know. He seeks the healer. Cannot find Him. Healing occurred; relationship pending. Physical sight precedes spiritual sight.
Religious authorities must examine. Control through interrogation.
Healing on Sabbath again. Kneading mud considered work. Legal violation charged.
Therefore the Pharisees also asked him how he had received his sight. He put mud on my eyes, and I washed, and now I see. Repetition. Same testimony. Consistent account. Truth stands under scrutiny.
Law versus miracle. Some judge by regulation; others by results. Division over Jesus persists.
Growing insight. Prophet: one who speaks for God. Theological understanding developing.
Denial of evidence. Parents summoned for verification. Bureaucratic obstruction.
Is this your son? Is this the one you say was born blind? How is it that now he can see? Three questions. Identity, condition, explanation sought. Witnesses interrogated.
We know he is our son, and we know he was born blind. Parents confirm facts. Identity and condition verified. Medical history established.
Fear prevents full testimony. Adult son must answer. Parents protect themselves.
Social death. Expelled from covenant community. Economic and relational ruin. Fear silences truth.
Terror drives deflection. Self-preservation over son's welfare.
Demand for confession. Give glory to God means confess the truth. They've predetermined Jesus is sinner. Verdict before evidence.
Personal certainty. He cannot judge Jesus' character. But he knows his own experience. Testimony rooted in transformation.
Looking for inconsistency. Searching for grounds to discredit.
Mockery. Bold challenge. He exposes their agenda. Do they truly seek truth or just ammunition?
Identity politics. They claim Moses, reject Jesus. False dichotomy. Jesus fulfills Moses.
This fellow: contemptuous. Moses validated; Jesus questioned. Origin determines authority in their view.
Religious experts confess ignorance. Uneducated man sees clearly. Power should reveal origin.
Sinners lack access. Righteous have God's ear. John 9:31, common belief cited.
No historical parallel. Unique sign. Isaiah 35:5 promised Messiah would do this.
Power indicates divine origin. Miracle validates mission. The beggar becomes theologian.
Congenital blindness blamed on sin. How dare the unlearned teach the scholars? Expulsion: excommunication fulfilled. He pays the cost his parents feared.
Divine initiative. Son of Man: Daniel 7:13, Messianic title. Faith question posed.
Who is He, sir? Tell me so that I may believe in Him. Willingness to believe. Openness. He lacks information, not disposition. Faith ready, awaits object.
First time he physically sees Jesus (healed eyes now functional). Identity disclosed. The Speaker is the Messiah.
Kyrios (κύριος), Lord. Worship appropriate only for God. Physical and spiritual sight achieved. Full conversion.
Spiritually blind receive sight. Physically seeing become spiritually blind. Jesus exposes and reverses. Judgment through revelation.
Self-assessment: we see. Implicit claim to spiritual sight.
Acknowledged blindness allows healing. Their claim to see prevents receiving sight. Pride blocks grace. Judgment rests on self-assessment.
